description:
You are acting as a DevOps engineer who has been asked to make a small adjustment to an **application-local firewall rule file** that lives in the user’s home directory.  
The project has its own non-root “mini-firewall” that simply parses a rules file; therefore no super-user privileges are needed.

Current project structure (already present when the session starts):

/home/user/firewall_task/  
├── firewall.rules     ← text file containing the current rule set  
└── README.txt         ← irrelevant for this task  

Initial contents of /home/user/firewall_task/firewall.rules

```
*filter
:INPUT DROP [0:0]
:FORWARD DROP [0:0]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
COMMIT
```

Business request
1. Allow inbound TCP traffic on port **8080** in addition to the already-allowed port 80.  
2. Produce a short debug log that lists every INPUT-chain **ACCEPT** rule (old and new) in a very specific, easy-to-parse format so that the ops team can feed it to their monitoring system.

Required final state
A. File “/home/user/firewall_task/firewall.rules” must contain **both** accept rules, in the order **80 first, 8080 second**, immediately before the line that reads `COMMIT`.  
B. Create a new file “/home/user/firewall_task/firewall_debug.log”.  
   • The file must contain exactly two lines.  
   • Each line must have the following fixed, space-separated columns (no extra whitespace and no leading/trailing spaces):  

     ACTION  PROTOCOL  PORT  

   • For this task, the only valid value for ACTION is the literal word `ALLOW`, the only valid value for PROTOCOL is the literal word `tcp`, and PORT must be the numeric port.  
   • Therefore, after a correct solution the file content must be **exactly**:

```
ALLOW tcp 80
ALLOW tcp 8080
```

Verification notes the automated grader will use  
1. It will read /home/user/firewall_task/firewall.rules and verify that the line  
   `-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8080 -j ACCEPT`  
   exists **and** is located after the 80-port rule but before the terminating `COMMIT`.  
2. It will read /home/user/firewall_task/firewall_debug.log and check that the file exists, is world-readable, and its entire content is byte-for-byte identical to the two-line block shown above (including the newline at the end of the second line).  
3. No other files should be modified or created.

Important constraints
• You do **not** need root privileges because the project treats the rules file as an ordinary text file.  
• Only a handful of standard shell utilities will be necessary (e.g., `echo`, `sed`, `printf`, `cat`, etc.).  
• Keep command count small (roughly three to four commands).  
• Do not hard-code absolute line numbers; add the new rule in relation to the `COMMIT` marker so the solution stays robust if earlier rules change in the future.  
• Ensure the debug log is created with default user permissions (read/write for the user, readable by group/others).  

After you finish, the two files should match the exact formats described above so that automated tests can validate them without ambiguity.
truth:
Expected final contents of /home/user/firewall_task/firewall.rules (including newlines exactly as shown):

*filter
:INPUT DROP [0:0]
:FORWARD DROP [0:0]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8080 -j ACCEPT
COMMIT

Expected final contents of /home/user/firewall_task/firewall_debug.log (including trailing newline on second line):
ALLOW tcp 80
ALLOW tcp 8080

Permissions: default 644 (-rw-r--r--) are sufficient for both files.

There must be no extra files in /home/user/firewall_task/ after completion.
